coredata.dc:title: Improved equivalent circuit coupled 3D thermal cylindrical lithium-ion battery model using a multi-partition heat generation and thermal resistance approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Auch, Marcus
Kuthada, Timo
Wagner, Andreas
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces an improved equivalent circuit coupled 3D thermal model, the Multi-Partition Heat Generation and Thermal Resistance (MPH-TR) Model, developed for cylindrical Lithium-Ion-Battery (LIB) cells to optimize battery thermal management with computational efficiency. The model offers detailed insights into temperature distributions within the jelly roll active material, crucial for assessing LIB cooling strategies. To develop the thermal model, a disassembly of the studied LIB cell is conducted, enabling the inclusion of cell components geometric data, electrical resistances and electrical insulation layers. By considering the detailed internal structure, the heat generation is separated between positive terminal and jelly roll. Additionally, the identified installed electrical insulation layers are simplified included as thermal resistances. The model’s validity is shown through extensive comparison with experimental data across various operating conditions (pre-conditioning, fast charge, slow charge, dynamic electrical cycle), demonstrating good agreement with root-mean-square errors (RMSE) below 0.8 K at all three measurement positions (cell mantle, positive and negative terminal). Compared to the state-of-the-art cylindrical approach, the MPH-TR model exhibited improved accuracy and provided deeper insights into the temperature distribution within the jelly roll. Furthermore, this study validated similar thermal behavior across multiple cells of the same LIB type, enabling upscaling of single cell models for LIB module or pack simulations.

head.ce:title: Improved equivalent circuit coupled 3D thermal cylindrical lithium-ion battery model using a multi-partition heat generation and thermal resistance approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces an improved equivalent circuit coupled 3D thermal model, the Multi-Partition Heat Generation and Thermal Resistance (MPH-TR) Model, developed for cylindrical Lithium-Ion-Battery (LIB) cells to optimize battery thermal management with computational efficiency. The model offers detailed insights into temperature distributions within the jelly roll active material, crucial for assessing LIB cooling strategies. To develop the thermal model, a disassembly of the studied LIB cell is conducted, enabling the inclusion of cell components geometric data, electrical resistances and electrical insulation layers. By considering the detailed internal structure, the heat generation is separated between positive terminal and jelly roll. Additionally, the identified installed electrical insulation layers are simplified included as thermal resistances. The model’s validity is shown through extensive comparison with experimental data across various operating conditions (pre-conditioning, fast charge, slow charge, dynamic electrical cycle), demonstrating good agreement with root-mean-square errors (RMSE) below 0.8 K at all three measurement positions (cell mantle, positive and negative terminal). Compared to the state-of-the-art cylindrical approach, the MPH-TR model exhibited improved accuracy and provided deeper insights into the temperature distribution within the jelly roll. Furthermore, this study validated similar thermal behavior across multiple cells of the same LIB type, enabling upscaling of single cell models for LIB module or pack simulations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
With the growing interest in the application of cylindrical Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIBs) for Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), accurate cylindrical cell modeling approaches have become an increasingly important research topic. Compared to other applications, such as in the energy storage sector, the use of cylindrical LIBs in BEVs poses demanding thermal requirements. Especially, the (extreme) fast charging scenarios need to be studied. In general, the higher the applied current, the greater the heat loss inside the LIB cell. To ensure the desired LIB operating temperature range of
25 − 40
∘
C
accurate thermal models are necessary to design reliable cooling systems [1]. Higher and lower temperatures can result into capacity fade, cycle life and safety issues of the battery pack [1–3]. However, not only the LIB average temperature but also the maximum temperature difference inside the LIB active material plays a significant role in the cooling design optimization, since it can increase the degradation of the LIB cells [4].

Developing precise thermal models for cylindrical LIB cells is a challenging task. In general, this task can be divided into two key aspects, which are the accurate modeling of both the heat conduction processes and the heat generation within the LIB. The heat conduction depends on various factors, including the components involved, cell structure and material properties. On the other hand, the heat generation relies on electrochemical processes occurring inside the cell. This results in the heat generation being dependent on temperature, current and hysteresis [5–7]. Consequently, thermal models must encompass a wide range of operating conditions, particularly for application in BEV cooling designs. The thermal model needs to accurately describe different use cases, as for example pre-heating scenarios, dynamic and fast charging cycles. Therefore, a multi-physics approach connecting the electrical and thermal model is necessary. For the electrical behavior of LIBs, three common modeling approaches exist. These include electrochemical/physical-based approaches (Single-Particle model/Pseudo 2D Model), empirical/data based models and the equivalent electrical circuit models (ECMs) [8]. Because of the high computational time of physical-based models and the immense data-sets necessary for data based models, ECMs are the main approach used for multi-physics electro-thermal simulations with the goal of designing adequate thermal management systems [7].

Cylindrical LIB cells exhibit highly non-uniform temperature distributions during operation [9,10]. This non-uniformity arises from various factors. Firstly, the current density within cylindrical LIB cells is not homogeneous, leading to uneven heat generation [9]. Furthermore, even under assumed homogeneous heat generation conditions the internal structure of LIB cells, characterized by rolled layers, results in an anisotropic thermal conductivity, inducing thermal gradients. The ohmic heat generation at the LIB cap and tabs contributes to the formation of temperature hot spots at these regions [9]. Operating conditions with high C-rates (fast charge) increase these thermal gradients, further intensified by the cooling system necessary for effective heat dissipation to prevent thermal safety issues [11]. These factors collectively contribute to inhomogeneous SOC distribution and degradation within LIB cells [12,13].

While the complexity of the thermal behavior inside cylindrical LIBs is well known, most thermal management and cooling design researchers use a simplified cylindrical modeling approach of the cell to investigate the cooling design and performance [14–17]. The cylindrical modeling approach assumes the generated heat inside the LIB as homogeneous volumetric heat source. These models are mostly validated by only comparing the cell mantle temperature at one measurement point or taking an average over the mantle surface as validation. Although the results obtained at this measurement position may align well with the model, this approach might not yield sufficient data regarding the temperature distribution within the cylindrical LIB cell. However, detailed geometric solving of the cylindrical cell (as done in electro-chemical modeling approaches) leads to unreasonable computational time, making it suitable only for short-term studies of single cells, primarily utilized by LIB cell developers. Hence, there is a lack of thermal models that bridge the gap between the simple cylindrical approach with an applied homogeneous volumetric heat source and detailed electro-chemical models, which primarily focus on the analysis of the jelly roll’s active material components.

There are several approaches to improve the results of the temperature distribution inside the LIB cell. One of the most promising is to consider the partition heat generation inside a cylindrical LIB cell, especially the separation between LIB jelly roll and cap. Yang et al. [10] proposed a multi-partition 3D geometry modeling of the LIB cell. For this purpose, they used the simplified Bernardi equation and separated the LIB ohmic electrical resistance of the equation into four parts. The four parts are the negative terminal, positive terminal, battery cap and jelly roll. In their study of an 18650 LIB cell, they found that the resistance of the positive and negative terminals appears to be negligible, while the battery cap resistance contributes to almost 50% of the total heat generation for
State of Charges
( SOCs )
above 20%. This results in higher temperatures at the battery cap compared to the mantle or negative terminal.

Dubey et al. [18] also studied a multi-partition heat generation approach for an 18650 LIB cell. In their study, the battery cap resistance accounted for 30% of the total heat generation at
SOC > 30 %
. However, they validated their model by only comparing the average surface temperature at different C-rates. Ahmad et al. [19] used a multi-partition heat generation approach, combining the positive terminal and cap heat generation partition together. This results in three heat generation parts, the positive terminal/cap, the negative terminal and the jelly roll. However, they also validated their model by only comparing the surface temperature of the LIB mantle. Heimes et al. [20] used a multi-partition heat generation approach without validating the cell model. They directly integrated it with various cooling architectures, making the results not comparable to the other studies mentioned.

Despite the presented non-uniform thermal behavior of cylindrical LIB cells, the current state-of-the-art modeling approach for investigating cooling designs remains the cylindrical approach [14–17]. The main advantages are that it is easy to understand, easy to implement and computational very efficient. The most promising approach, the presented multi-partition heat generation model, is often unsuitable for module or pack simulations due to the detailed resolution of the cap and tab components, which significantly increases computational time. Therefore, this study aims to develop an efficient cylindrical cell model using a simplified multi-partition heat generation approach. Furthermore, the cell disassembly is used to ascertain the electrical resistances of the LIB cap and tabs, as well as the thermal resistances arising from the installed electrical insulation layers. This enhances the model’s detail and accuracy in predicting the thermal behavior within the LIB cell, while keeping computational demands manageable.

Furthermore, as previously discussed, there is a general lack of utilized measurement points for the validation of cylindrical electro-thermal cell models. This study aims to increase the validity by not only using the cell mantle but also the positive and negative terminal. Additionally, in the literature, electro-thermal models are predominantly validated for different constant current profiles. By incorporating the most common scenarios of BEV cycles, such as pre-heating, fast charging, slow charging and highly dynamic discharge cycles, the developed model can undergo comprehensive validation. Finally, to illustrate the applicability of the developed validated cell model for module or even pack simulations, multiple cells are tested using the same electrical cycle to compare thermal behavior and validate the scaling from single cell tests to module simulations.
In the following, the required fundamentals of thermodynamics and electro-chemistry are provided.

ECMs are the common modeling approach chosen for electro-thermal simulations due to the good accuracy while maintaining reasonable computational time. A Dual Polarization model (DPM) considering the parameter dependencies of hysteresis, current and temperature is the best choice for thermal simulations regarding the predicted heat generation and hence, for coupled electro-thermal LIB simulations [5–7]. In the experimental setup section, the electro-thermal measurement, the disassembly of the studied LIB cell and the determination of the electrical resistances of specific cell components are presented. For this study the cylindrical LG INR21700 M50T LIB cell has been chosen. This cell uses lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC 811) as active cathode material and graphite-silicon as anode. The voltage range is specified as
U = 2 . 5 − 4 . 2
V
, with a nominal voltage of 3.63V and a nominal energy of
18 . 2
Wh
[29].

To examine the defined electrical cycles as well as voltage and current measurements a Basytec HPS battery tester system is used. The measurement range for the voltage is
0 − 6
V
and for the current, it is
+
−
60
A
. The voltage measurement accuracy is 1 mV and the current accuracy is 0.05
% Full Scale. Moreover, the temperature around the studied LIB cells is regulated by a Weiss Umwelttechnik GmbH climate chamber. Finally, the cell sample is put inside a cylindrical cell holder with integrated voltage measurement by Feinmetall. All temperature measurements are performed with a NI-DAQ data acquisition system.

The experimental setup for the electro-thermal measurements is shown in Fig. 1. Self-adhering type K thermocouples have been used to record the temperature. The thermocouples have been calibrated resulting in an accuracy of 0.1K [30]. In total, three different measurement positions have been chosen, consisting of the cell mantle
T
c
, the positive
T
pt
and negative terminal
T
nt
. For a precise positioning in the center of the positive and negative terminals, the self-adhering thermocouples were cut into a triangular shape. Furthermore, to eliminate potential heat conduction through the cell holder, the only contact to the cell holder is made via the electrical contacts. Between the bottom side of the LIB and the cell holder there is a 10 mm air gap.

To gain insights to the inner structure, the LIB has been disassembled. This allows the measurement of the geometrical dimensions of every LIB component. The primary focus of the disassembly is on the cap and tab regions of the LIB cell. The detailed internal structure of the jelly roll is of secondary importance since it is modeled as a homogeneous region. In Fig. 2 all disassembled components of the studied cell are shown. The measured geometrical parameters of the main LIB components are presented in Table 1.

To study the internal LIB structure, the chosen tab design of the LIB cell is analyzed first. A single tab design on the positive as well as on the negative side is used in this cell, agreeing with the results presented by Baazouzi et al. [31]. The positive tab is connected to both the positive terminal and the positive current collector in the jelly roll. Inside the jelly roll, kapton tape is used to electrically isolate the tab from all other components. Furthermore, the tab is mounted on the positive current collector with an electrical conductive glue. A small part of the outer ring negative current collector is connected to the negative tab on the curved surface of the cell casing. The negative tab follows the casing structure to the negative terminal (bottom side) of the LIB cell, as can be seen in Fig. 2.

Additionally, the disassembly enables analyzing the electrical insulation layers of the LIB, which are used to prevent an internal short circuit. In total, three layers could be identified. There is a layer between the jelly roll and the cap, a layer between the jelly roll and the bottom side of the casing and finally, there is a thicker layer of electrical insulation between the cap and the casing.

The internal structure of the jelly roll is a classical cylindrical cell design consisting of five layers. These are the positive current collector, the active cathode material, the separator, the active anode material and the negative current collector.

The disassembly of the cell has been used to investigate the electrical resistance of the LIB cap and tabs. For this purpose, a simple electrical circuit experimental setup has been built. A constant current is set using an EA-PS2332-025 current source machine. By inserting a precision resistor and measuring the voltage across it, the current value can be calculated by Eq. (13). The electrical circuit is closed by connecting the LIB cap and measuring the voltage between points both before and after the LIB cap. By utilizing Eq. (13) and having the precise current value and the measured voltage, it is possible to calculate the electrical resistance
R
el,cap
of the LIB cap. An important consideration is finding a balance between a sufficiently high current for accurate voltage measurements and a low enough current to prevent self-heating of the LIB cap. The results are shown in Table 2 and the electrical resistance of the cap has been measured as
R
el,cap
= 0 . 85
m Ω
.

Steinhardt et al. [32] measured the cap electrical resistance for the same cell as
R
el,cap
= 2 . 4
m Ω
. The results of this study are 65% lower. However, Steinhardt et al. [32] soldered the electrical circuit on the cap, which could have increased the measured value. Because the positive tab can be easily isolated from the cell, geometric measurements were utilized to accurately calculate its electrical resistance with Eq. (14). The positive tab is manufactured out of aluminum having an electrical resistivity of
ρ

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the equivalent circuit coupled 3D thermal MPH-TR model
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the development process of the multi-partition heat generation and thermal resistance approach is presented, referred to as the MPH-TR model. The electrical model is briefly introduced as it is implemented using state-of-the-art methods. The overall procedure is shown in Table 3 and extensively described in the subsequent sections.

This study uses a DPM, as described in Section 2.2.1, with parameter dependencies of hysteresis, current and temperature included. The measurement points for the DPM are set using 5% steps over the whole LIB SOC-range. For current steps, C-rates of 0.2, 0.5, 1 and 2C are selected. As for temperature points, temperatures of
T = 5 , 25 , 45
∘
C
are used. The characterization process consists of an activation, capacity, Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) and entropic coefficient test procedure, as explained in detail in a previously published article by the authors in [5]. Five cells were tested using the arithmetic mean of the capacities and all ECM parameters as input for the DPM. The average capacity of all five cells at
T = 25
∘
C
is at
C
avg
= 4 . 74
Ah
. The ECM can be made available upon request. Exemplary, the resulting ECM parameters for all three temperature data points during a discharge pulse of 0.5C are shown in Appendix A.3.

There are two important aspects for the development of the novel 3D thermal model. Firstly, a multi-partition heat generation approach offers more detailed insights into the internal LIB temperature distribution. Secondly, by modeling the electrical insulation layers as thermal resistances, the 3D thermal model is simplified, ensuring reasonable computational time. For this reason, the detailed insights obtained from the disassembly of the LIB cell, as discussed in Section 3.2, are utilized. Moreover, the electrical resistances of the cap, positive tab and jelly roll, as discussed in Section 3.3, are taken into account. With these considerations, the 3D thermal model is introduced, as shown in Fig. 3.

The generated heat is calculated by the electrical model with the Bernardi Eq. (18). For this purpose, the DPM parameters enable calculating the irreversible losses and the entropic coefficient term enables calculating the reversible losses. Both loss terms are dependent on the temperature, resulting in a multi-physics coupled electro-thermal simulation. The coupling is done for every simulation time step
Δ t
. The thermal model provides the current temperature to the electrical model, while the electrical model supplies the current heat generation rate to the thermal model.

Robinson et al. [33] have shown, that spatially allocating the ohmic resistances (and hence, heat sources) inside the LIB is essential to capture the spatial temperature distribution inside a cylindrical LIB cell. By using this approach, the calculated heat of the whole battery cell unit
Q
̇
tot
can be split into
(19)

Q

whereas the generated heat is separated into the jellyroll
Q
̇

, cap
Q

, positive
Q

and negative terminal
Q
̇

. Yang et al. [34] investigated the influence of these four heat generation sources and found that the heat generation from the positive and negative terminal is negligible compared to that of the jelly roll and cap.

However, as shown in Table 2, this conclusion is not accurate for the studied cell. The electrical resistance of the cap
R
el,cap
accounts for only 2.66% of the total electrical resistance of the whole battery cell (studied cell:
R
el,cell
≈ 32
m Ω
in the SOC-range from 20 to 100%). Nevertheless, at high C-rates, both the literature and this study consistently observe the highest temperatures at the LIB cap. In contrast to the findings of Yang et al. [34], the electrical resistance of the cap could not be identified as the source of this phenomenon. However, in the experimental setup designed to measure the electrical resistance of the cap, Yang et al. [34] included at least a portion of the positive tab in the measurement procedure. Consequently, an investigation into the elevated temperature in the cap region at high C-rates, which could result from the inclusion of the positive tab’s heat generation in the cap region, is conducted.

As shown in Table 2, the positive tab accounts for 17.75% of the total LIB electrical resistance in the SOC-range from
20 − 100 %
. The combined heat generation of positive tab and cap is responsible for 20.41% and therefore, of the total generated heat inside the LIB cell. Considering the small volume and mass of the cap compared to the jelly roll, the cumulative heat generation from both the positive tab and cap can explain the observed temperature increase at the positive terminal during high C-rates.

As mentioned in Section 3.2, the positive tab is electrically isolated by using kapton tape from all components inside the jelly roll, except for the positive current collector. The low thermal conductivity of kapton tape (
λ = 0 . 12 − 0 . 2
W
m
K
) also thermally isolates the positive tab from the other components inside the jelly roll. While the positive tab is soldered to the cap, the disassembly showed that it is adhered to the positive current collector by the use of a conductive adhesive. Those adhesives can have a high electrical conductivity but a low thermal conductivity (
λ = 0 . 5 − 3
W
m
K
). Consequently, this study makes the assumption, that the heat generated by the positive tab flows into the positive terminal/cap of the LIB. Due to its low electrical resistance and large contact area with the cell casing, the heat generation from the negative tab is neglected as its own source of heat generation and is instead included in the overall heat generation of the jelly roll. This enables the geometrical setup of the cylindrical LIB as shown in Fig. 3 (b), dividing the LIB into three regions: the casing, the jelly roll and the positive terminal, simplifying the detailed structure of the LIB cap.

For the application of the developed thermal model, the thermal properties of all three regions need to be determined. They consist of the densities ρ , the specific heat capacities
c
p
and the thermal conductivities λ . Furthermore, the electrical insulation components are not geometrically modeled. Instead, they are simplified as thermal resistances
R
th
as shown in Fig. 3 (c). This saves valuable meshing and computational time while maintaining the internal structure of the studied LIB. Since the developed 3D model will be compared to a standard cylindrical approach, the thermal properties for the cylindrical approach are additionally presented, labeled as ‘Whole Cell’.

The first thermal property discussed is the density of the regions, as shown in Table 4. To determine the densities, the masses of the jelly roll, positive terminal and casing have been measured. By taking the volume of the corresponding regions of the developed model, the densities of the regions can be calculated. As a result, the correct thermal behavior can be maintained although the structure of the positive terminal and jelly roll is simplified. The resulting values used in the simulation are shown in Table 4.

The specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity values are shown in Table 5. These values are either measured, calculated, estimated or obtained from the literature. Since the LIB jelly roll has an anisotropic thermal conductivity, it is separated into a radial thermal conductivity
λ
r,jr
and an axial/circumferential conductivity
λ
z,ϕ,jr
. The positive terminal and the casing have a constant thermal conductivity in all spatial directions.

Finally, the thermal resistances of the developed model are shown in Table 6. The corresponding spatial positions are marked in Fig. 3 (c). For the thermal resistance between the positive terminal and the jelly roll

. All thermal resistances are calculated by Eq. (4). The resulting values are assigned to the corresponding interfaces in the thermal model.

The boundary conditions are set using a superposition of heat convection and radiation [22]. The heat transfer coefficients h for radiation and natural convection can be calculated using the correlations provided in Section 2.1. Additionally, the ambient temperature is an important parameter for the application of the correlations. Consequently, the ambient temperature
T
amb
has been measured during all test cycles and imported to the simulation model to establish the boundary conditions. The boundary conditions have been further distinguished between mantle, positive terminal and negative terminal to consider potential varying temperature differences. The natural convection Nusselt correlation given in Eq. (6) is used at the mantle surface, while the positive and negative terminal surfaces are calculated with Eq. (8). Inside the LIB cell, all walls are assumed to be adiabatic.

One important aspect of the boundary conditions is on the positive terminal side. Due to the simplified model depicted in Fig. 3 (c), the outer surface of the positive terminal is represented as a flat surface. However, regarding the real LIB cell, there exists a gap between the upper thin plate and the remainder of the cap, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Therefore, considering the additional surface area due to the cap design, the boundary condition for the heat flux q through the positive terminal

All simulations are conducted with the commercially available CFD-solver Star-CCM+ 2302 [39]. The solver uses a finite volume method to solve the energy equation (1). While the jelly roll and positive terminal region are discretized with a polyhedral mesh, the casing is relatively thin compared to the other two regions. As a result, the thin mesher has been used in the casing region to adequately capture the heat conduction behavior. A minimum of three thin layer discretization cells has been chosen in all spatial directions. Furthermore, Star-CCM+ enables electro-thermal multi-physics simulations by providing a battery model. For the battery model, the ECM parameters, the start SOC, the capacity and the entropic coefficient need to be provided in Look-Up-Tables (LUTs). The coupling of the temperature dependent ECM parameters and the heat generation dependent thermal model is integrated into the used solver. Electrical cycles can be simulated as defined by the user. The SOC is estimated by Ampere-hour method. The implicit unsteady model combined with a segregated solid energy model is used to simulate the dynamic electro-thermal behavior of the studied LIB cell. All simulations have been executed with a time step of
Δ t = 1
s
and four inner iterations per time step [39].

In the following, the developed model is validated by experimental measurements. For this purpose, a pre-heating, fast charge, slow charge and World Harmonized Light Vehicle Test Cycle (WLTC) scenario are presented. In addition, the developed electro-thermal model is compared to a state-of-the-art cylindrical approach used in module simulations, assessing both accuracy and computational time. Finally, four additional cells are tested to assess the similarity of their thermal behavior.

A pre-heating scenario without applying any current to the LIB cell is studied to be able to validate the thermal model and its boundary conditions first. The scenario is defined by soaking the studied cell for 3h at
5
∘
C
. Then, the climate chamber is turned off to ensure natural convection boundary conditions and its door is immediately opened exposing the LIB cell to the environmental temperature of the room. There are two scenarios studied, which consist of Case 1: The LIB hanging in the middle of the climate chamber (an experimental setup as shown in Appendix A.2) and Case 2: The LIB being positioned inside the cylindrical cell holder (as it will be the case for the electrical cycles). The thermocouples are attached to the center of the cell mantle, the positive and negative terminal, as shown in Fig. 1. Furthermore, one additional thermocouple is placed inside the climate chamber close to the LIB cell in the air to measure the ambient temperature
T
amb
.

The emissivity value of the LIB surface is taken from Huang et al. [40] as
ϵ
casing
= 0 . 45
, which lies in the literature value range for the emissivity of steel. The analyzed temperature increase range is from 5 to
15
∘
C
, representing a common pre conditioning target temperature for a fast charge case.

The scenario of the LIB hanging in the middle of the climate chamber (Case 1) is discussed first. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) the temperature increases similarly at all three measurement points. It can be observed, that the temperature increases the fastest at the positive terminal, then the negative terminal and lastly, the mantle. This is elucidated by analyzing the heat conduction and convection phenomena within the LIB cell. The temperature measurement location at the positive terminal is subject to increased heat convection, primarily because of its exposed surface in comparison to the mantle and the negative terminal. Furthermore, the electrical insulation limits heat conduction to the jelly roll, resulting in the observed fastest temperature increase at the positive terminal. The difference between negative terminal and mantle can also be explained by the electrical insulation layer, which is located between negative terminal and jelly roll.

Fig. 4 (a) and Table 7 are used to discuss the simulation results. With
RMSE < 0 . 5
K
at all three measurement positions the simulation and experimental results show a very good agreement. However, a difference between the measurement and simulation can be observed in the early time frame. The reason can be identified by considering the test procedure. After the LIB has been soaked for 3 h, the climate chamber is turned off and the climate chamber door is immediately opened. This door opening procedure can cause a temporary fluid movement resulting in forced convection and therefore, increased heat transfer around the LIB. Furthermore, the pre-heating case simulation is also used to conduct a mesh convergence study by applying three different mesh base sizes in the simulation. The results are shown in Appendix Table A.1.

In Fig. 4 (b), the results of the Case 2 pre-heating scenario are shown, where the placement of the LIB has been changed to inside the cell holder. In this case, the LIB does not exhibit ideal natural convection and radiation boundary conditions because its lower side is faced towards the cell holder’s top surface. The setup can be seen in Fig. 1. As a result, the heat transfer is overestimated by the applied correlations. To consider this influence, a correction of the natural convection and radiation boundary conditions is proposed. The correction factor
BC
corr
is applied to all boundary conditions as
(32)
BC
corr, i
=
BC
corr
BC
i
with an empirical determined correction factor of
BC
corr
= 0 . 85
. Hence, the changed positioning of the LIB cell from the middle of the climate chamber to the cell holder decreases the heat transfer by 15%. The results for the simulation with and without the empirically determined correction factor are shown in Table 7. The corrected boundary conditions are used for all electro-thermal simulations.

In total, there are three different electrical cycles studied for the validation of the developed electro-thermal model, consisting of a fast charge, slow charge and dynamic WLTC scenario. The fast charge scenario is selected to represent the most demanding thermal use case. The slow charge scenario represents potential charging at home. Finally, to validate the developed model for dynamic cycles, a current profile derived from the WLTC, as published by the authors in [5], is used.

The overall test procedure steps are the same for all three cycles. The LIB is placed inside the cell holder and soaked for 3h at the defined ambient temperature of
T
amb
= 25
∘
C
to achieve chemical and thermal equilibrium. Then, a full constant current constant voltage charge cycle is examined according to the manufacturers data sheet [29]. Subsequently, the cell is fully discharged to reach an SOC of 0% and allowed to rest for 30min. Next, the initial SOC is established by applying a current of 0.5C for the required duration. In the case of the two charging scenarios, a duration of 24min is chosen to achieve an initial SOC of 20%. For the WLTC, the duration is set at 30min to reach an initial SOC of 25%. The LIB is soaked again for 3h to achieve chemical and thermal equilibrium. Finally, the climate chamber is turned off to prevent an influence of the climate chamber ventilator on the temperature measurements and the studied electrical cycle is examined. The resulting SOC over time results for all three cycles are shown in Appendix A.4.

The fast charge scenario is defined as a constant current constant voltage electrical cycle. The constant current frame consists of a 2C charge until the defined end voltage of
U = 4 . 15
V
is reached. While the cell manufacturer defines the maximum voltage at
4 . 2
V
, it is common in BEVs to further limit this voltage to reduce potential degradation effects [41]. The constant voltage frame is applied by charging the cell with a constant voltage of
U = 4 . 15
V
. The experimental voltage and current cycle measured is shown in Fig. 5 (b).

In Fig. 5 (a) the experimental results of the fast charge case at all three measurement positions is shown. Therefore, the temperature increase
Δ T
in relation to the start temperature
T
start
= 25
∘
C
is shown. The maximum temperature increase can be observed shortly after the constant current time frame at the positive terminal measurement point, with
Δ
T
exp,max,pt
( t = 623
s )
= 38 . 90
K
. Additionally, in the constant current time frame (2C), the temperature increase at the positive terminal is significantly higher compared to the mantle and negative terminal measurement positions. At the mantle and negative terminal the overall temperature increase behavior is similar. However, at the negative terminal the temperatures are slightly lower compared to the mantle. At all three temperature measurement positions, the temperature decreases after the constant current time frame is completed.

To ensure that the elevated temperature at the positive terminal is not attributed to the electrical contact system, the cell holder manufacturer Feinmetall’s data sheet [42] and a study on electrical contact resistance by Wassiliadis et al. [43] are considered. According to the data sheet, the temperature of the electrical contact pin does not increase by more than 1K with applied currents up to 10A. In this study, the maximum current is
I
max
= 9 . 5
A
. The chosen contacting system, featuring three electrical contact pins, exhibits a low electrical contact resistance comparable to that achieved by laser bonding [43].

As shown in Table 7, the mean error at the positive terminal
ME
pt
= − 0 . 42
K
indicates very good agreement. However, it is advisable not to rely solely on it for validation, because the temperature is underestimated in the 2C time frame and then overestimated when the C-rate drops due to voltage limitation. This tendency can be explained by the simplified thermal modeling approach. The temperature measurement is done on the thin upper steel plate of the positive terminal, as shown in Fig. 1. Due to its low mass, the actual LIB cap upper steel plate responds more rapidly to heat generation and heat convection compared to the simplified cap region of the developed model. Despite resulting in a
RMSE
pt
= 2 . 47
K
, the model captures qualitatively the overall thermal behavior at the positive terminal. At the mantle and negative terminal the simulation results agree very well with the experimental measurements, having
RMSE
c
= 0 . 45
K
and
RMSE
nt
= 0 . 75
K
. The differences at these measurement points can be due to the electrical model, which slightly over- and underestimates the voltage over the whole fast charge cycle, as shown in Fig. 5 (b). However, the voltage behavior and hence, the predicted heat generation rates are accurately modeled with a
RMSE
U
of
19
mV
. Additionally, the calculated heat transfer coefficients for the boundary conditions of the fast charge scenario are shown in Appendix A.5.

Consistent with the fast charge scenario, the slow charge scenario is also defined as a constant current constant voltage cycle. It only differs in the defined applied current for the constant current frame, which is set as 0.33C. This results in longer charging times to get to the constant voltage time frame compared to the fast charge, as can be seen in Fig. 5 (d).

In Fig. 5 (c), the experimental temperature measurement results for the slow charge case are presented. The overall temperature increase is lower compared to the fast charge case, primarily because of the reduced heat generation resulting from the lower current. Furthermore, the difference between the three measurement points (mantle, positive terminal, and negative terminal) decreased to a maximum of 0.5K.

The MEs and RMSEs for the slow charge case are
< 0 . 5
K
at all three measurement positions showing very good agreement with the experimental measurement. The differences in the time frame between 1000 and 3000s is assumed to rely on the entropic coefficient. At lower C-rates the reversible heat generation term plays a more significant role compared to higher C-rates. The voltage is accurately modeled with a
RMSE
U
of
14
mV
.

To ensure validation under variable current conditions, the dynamic WLTC scenario has also been examined. The results are shown in Fig. 5 (e) and (f). A highly sensible temperature increase behavior resulting of the applied current pulses can be observed at the positive terminal. Minor fluctuations can be identified on the negative terminal due to the heat generation of the negative tab. However, both at the mantle and the negative terminal, the temperature increases over time, except for the last 100s. In general, the results show that the WLTC scenario is not critical from a thermal management standpoint as the overall mantle temperature increase is
< 1 . 5
K and maximum temperature increase at the positive terminal is
< 3
K.

The overall accuracy compared to the measurements is very good at all three positions with
RMSE < 0 . 3
K
, as can be seen in Table 7. The difference of the peaks at the positive terminal can be explained with the same reasons as for the fast charge case. As a result of the thin plate of the cap, whereas the thermocouple is placed, the temperature response can be faster compared to the simplified model used in this study. However, the model follows the overall temperature behavior at the positive terminal very well. The developed electro-thermal model is also able to simulate highly dynamic drive cycles with a
RMSE
U
of
4
mV
.

To show the benefits of increased model accuracy while maintaining reasonable computational time, a comparison of a state-of-the-art cylindrical approach to the developed model is conducted. A focus in the discussion is set on the fast charge scenario, since this is the most challenging electrical cycle for the thermal management design. However, the slow charge case is also taken into account to assess the improved modeling accuracy over multiple cycles. A comparison of the model development process for both approaches is shown in Table 3. In the cylindrical approach, the thermal properties labeled as ‘Whole Cell’ in Section 4.3 are employed.

The comparison is done by evaluating the RMSEs of both approaches compared to the experimental measurement data points, as shown in Fig. 6 and Table 8. Regarding the fast charge case, the RMSEs of the MPH-TR model are lower compared to the cylindrical approach at all three measurement points. At the mantle the RMSE is 0.63K lower and at the negative terminal the RMSE is decreased by 0.34K. The highest RMSE decrease, which is due to the multi-partition heat generation approach in the developed model can be seen at the positive terminal with −5.17K. Furthermore, a comparison between the two modeling approaches has been done for the slow charge case. Due to the already low errors the RMSE is decreased between 0.02 and 0.09K compared to the cylindrical approach. Consequently, the MPH-TR model shows more accurate results not only for the fast charge but also for slow charge scenarios. However, at lower C-rates the thermal model improvement is not as important since the thermal gradients inside the LIB are generally much lower compared to higher C-rates.

Two of the most important parameters in thermal management design are the maximum jelly roll temperature
T
max,jr
and the maximum temperature difference inside the jelly roll
Δ
T
max,jr,cylinder
. The maximum jelly roll temperature is underestimated by 0.84K in the cylindrical approach (
T
max,jr
= 59 . 66
∘
C
) compared to the MPH-TR model (
T
max,jr
= 60 . 50
∘
C
). Regarding the maximum temperature difference inside the jelly roll, the cylindrical approach (
Δ
T
max,jr
= 1 . 63
K
) underestimates the value by 3.14K compared to the MPH-TR model (
Δ
T
max,jr
= 4 . 77
K
). Both values are crucial for the appropriate design of the cooling channels and required coolant inlet conditions. The reason for the temperature difference can be analyzed with Fig. 7.

The temperature increase
Δ T
compared to the start temperature
T
( t = 0 )
= 25
∘
C
is shown at four different time steps
t = 5 , 300 ,
620 , 1800
s
of the fast charge case. The maximum temperature increase of the simulation is found at the positive terminal position
Δ
T
max,pt
= 38 . 66
K
. At all four time steps, it can be seen that the maximum temperature difference inside the jelly roll
Δ
T
max,jr
is higher with the MPH-TR compared to the cylindrical approach. This is due to a temperature gradient in the axial axis of the cylindrical LIB implied by the multi-partition heat generation approach combined with the thermal resistances representing the electrical insulation layers. On the other hand, in the cylindrical approach the thermal gradient can be identified from the inside to the outside of the cell in radial direction due to the applied boundary conditions.

In the study by Li et al. [44], the same LG21700 M50T cell is used. They demonstrated a maximum temperature difference of
Δ
T
max,jr
= 7 . 5
K
resulting of a temperature gradient in the axial direction of the cylindrical LIB cell. The results surpass the maximum difference simulated in this paper (
Δ
T
max,jr
= 4 . 8
K
). However, they studied a full discharge from 100 to 0% SOC with a C-rate of 1.5. Low (
< 15
%) and high (
> 95
%) SOC-ranges lead to higher internal resistances in the LIB, leading to increased heat generation. The conclusion drawn is that the novel approach presented in this study aligns well with the findings of Li et al. [44]. Consequently, it is better suited to represent the thermal behavior of cylindrical LIB cells than the cylindrical approach. This increased accuracy in the temperature distribution and thermal behavior of the LIB can enable better optimization of cooling concepts for LIB modules.

Considering the detailed knowledge obtained from cell disassembly, the thermal transmittance in the axial direction can be calculated using Eq. (3). This calculation utilizes the thermal conductivities of the axial electrical insulation layers, the negative terminal casing, the jelly roll and the positive terminal. The resulting axial thermal conductivity equivalent is determined as
k
z , ϕ
= 3 . 54
W
m
K
. When this value is applied in the simulation, it results in an increase in the maximum temperature difference inside the jelly roll for the fast charge case, reaching
Δ
T
max,jr
= 4 . 47
K
. This temperature variation is comparable to the MPH-TR model results, as shown in Appendix A.6. However, it is worth noting that while the absolute value is similar to the MPH-TR model, the thermal behavior is not accurately modeled. The temperature gradient in the cylindrical approach occurs with a time delay and still arises from variations between the LIB cell core and its outer surface, rather than in the axial direction.

Finally, the computational time is an important parameter for module simulations. To accurately evaluate the computational time, the discretization of the two approaches should be similar. Therefore, the cylindrical model has a total cell number of
n = 53037
which is close to the medium mesh cell number shown in Table A.1 of the MPH-TR model with
n = 53215
. Comparing the CPU hours for the fast charge case, it is increased by 39%. This entails a reasonable increase in computational effort, while providing more accurate results and a deeper understanding of the maximum temperature difference inside the LIB. Consequently, the MPH-TR model can be employed in module simulations within a reasonable computational time frame.

For thermal modeling of BEV modules, multiple LIB cells need to be studied simultaneously. To ensure that scaling a module or even pack simulation up from single cell validated models, four additional cells have been tested for the fast charge and WLTC scenario. Here, the assumption has been made, that measuring at one position (
T
c
) is enough to show the similarity of the thermal behavior. The temperature measurement results for the fast charge and WLTC scenario of all five cells are shown in Fig. 8. The previously discussed and validated experimental data is included as Cell 1.

The overall thermal behavior is similar for all five tested cells. This is valid for the static fast charge as well as for the highly dynamic WLTC scenario. In the fast charge scenario, the maximum average absolute deviation compared to the mean temperature (
T
c,mean
) is 0.67K for Cell 1. In the case of the WLTC, the maximum average deviation is 0.05K also for Cell 1. Comparing the deviations with the RMSEs of the simulation of
< 0 . 80
K
, they are in a similar range. Therefore, the simulation offers an accuracy comparable to the variation observed from cell to cell. As a result, the upscaling from single cell validated models to module simulations can be examined. To ensure the overall applicability of the proposed MPH-TR model, further studies should be conducted using cells of different chemistry, size and capacity.

In this study, an improved ECM coupled 3D thermal model is proposed, that incorporates a multi-partition heat generation approach and accounts for thermal resistances due to the electrical insulation layers. Referred to as the MPH-TR model, it is validated against experimental data at three measurement points, including the center of the cell mantle, the positive terminal and the negative terminal. Four scenarios are investigated: pre-heating, fast charging, slow charging and WLTC. The MPH-TR model shows good agreement at all measurement points for all studied scenarios, with
RMSE < 0 . 8
K
except for in the fast charge case at the positive terminal (
RMSE
pt
= 2 . 47
K
). Furthermore, it achieves improved accuracy compared to a standard cylindrical approach while maintaining reasonable computational efficiency. This is particularly important given the oversimplification of the cylindrical approach, which neglects the complex structure of cylindrical LIB cells. This leads to significant discrepancies in the jelly roll temperature prediction, potentially resulting in insufficient design conclusions regarding cooling channel architectures or required coolant inlet conditions.

The primary enhancement is attributed to the combined effect of the multi-partition heat generation approach and the consideration of electrical insulation layers as thermal resistances. This results in a temperature gradient in the axial direction of the LIB, reflecting realistic thermal behavior across all scenarios comparable to more complex models.

Finally, the thermal behavior across five cells in both fast charge and WLTC scenarios at the mantle measurement point is demonstrated to be similar, facilitating upscaling from single-cell validated models to LIB module simulations. The MPH-TR model demonstrates a significant influence of the tab design on the thermal gradients within the jelly roll. Therefore, this model can serve as a valuable tool for thermal design studies aimed at optimizing the tab, cap or casing structure of future LIB cells.

Comparing the results of this study with the latest industry trends, both recommend a transition to a multiple tab or tabless design. Particularly for fast and extreme fast charging cases with high C-rates, dividing the current into multiple shorter tabs can reduce positive tab ohmic heat generation, thereby decreasing thermal gradients in the axial direction of the LIB cell. Additionally, the findings of this study indicate that the fast charge scenario represents the most challenging cycle in terms of maximum jelly roll temperature difference and overall maximum LIB temperature.

To address this challenge, employing the MPH-TR model and optimizing the thermal management cooling design for the fast charge case can lead to a reduction in these values. This optimization may involve placing cooling channels as close to the positive terminal as possible to minimize axial direction thermal gradients.

The proposed development process of the electro-thermal model can be applied to all cylindrical cells, including those with other active cathode material chemistry (such as LFP or NCA) as well as larger tabless 46xx formats. Moreover, the coupled electrical model could be modified to a porous electrode theory based 1D model assuming uniform electrochemical reactions over the electrodes. Future investigations are planned to utilize the proposed MPH-TR model for cooling design optimization studies in a cylindrical BEV module simulation.

Marcus Auch: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Timo Kuthada: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Andreas Wagner: Supervision, Resources, Project administration.
